instruction: How do I use criterion for benchmarking Rust code properly? I want statistically meaningful results, not just timing a loop.
output:
<think>
criterion-rs is the gold standard for Rust benchmarking. Unlike simple `Instant::now()` timing, it runs the benchmark many times, applies statistical analysis (bootstrap resampling), detects outliers, and compares against previous runs to detect regressions.

Key practices: (1) Use `black_box()` to prevent the compiler from optimizing away your benchmark code. Without it, LLVM might realize the result is unused and eliminate the computation entirely. (2) Use `iter_batched` for benchmarks that need setup per iteration (like creating a Vec to sort). (3) Group benchmarks with `benchmark_group` for comparison. (4) Set measurement time appropriately — short functions need more iterations.

criterion generates HTML reports with charts showing distribution, regression analysis, and outlier detection. It also saves previous results so you can run `cargo bench` and automatically see if performance regressed.

For async benchmarks, use `criterion::async_executor::FuturesExecutor` or tokio's runtime. For benchmarks that allocate, pair with `dhat` or `jemalloc` stats to measure allocation counts.
</think>

```rust
// Cargo.toml:
// [dev-dependencies]
// criterion = { version = "0.5", features = ["html_reports"] }
//
// [[bench]]
// name = "my_benchmarks"
// harness = false

use criterion::{
    black_box, criterion_group, criterion_main,
    Criterion, BenchmarkId, BatchSize,
};
use std::collections::HashMap;

// Function under benchmark
fn fibonacci(n: u64) -> u64 {
    match n {
        0 => 0,
        1 => 1,
        _ => fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2),
    }
}

fn fibonacci_iterative(n: u64) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        let tmp = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = tmp;
    }
    a
}

// Basic benchmark
fn bench_fibonacci(c: &mut Criterion) {
    c.bench_function("fib_recursive_20", |b| {
        b.iter(|| fibonacci(black_box(20)))  // black_box prevents optimization
    });
}

// Comparison group: same function, different inputs
fn bench_fibonacci_comparison(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("fibonacci");

    for n in [10, 15, 20, 25].iter() {
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("recursive", n),
            n,
            |b, &n| b.iter(|| fibonacci(black_box(n))),
        );
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("iterative", n),
            n,
            |b, &n| b.iter(|| fibonacci_iterative(black_box(n))),
        );
    }
    group.finish();
}

// Benchmark with per-iteration setup (sorting)
fn bench_sorting(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("sorting");

    for size in [100, 1000, 10000].iter() {
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("vec_sort", size),
            size,
            |b, &size| {
                // iter_batched: setup runs before each iteration
                b.iter_batched(
                    || {
                        let mut v: Vec<u64> = (0..size).rev().collect();
                        v
                    },
                    |mut v| {
                        v.sort_unstable();  // measured
                        v
                    },
                    BatchSize::SmallInput,
                );
            },
        );
    }
    group.finish();
}

// Benchmark HashMap operations
fn bench_hashmap(c: &mut Criterion) {
    c.bench_function("hashmap_insert_1000", |b| {
        b.iter(|| {
            let mut map = HashMap::with_capacity(1000);
            for i in 0..1000u64 {
                map.insert(black_box(i), black_box(i * 2));
            }
            map
        });
    });
}

criterion_group!(
    benches,
    bench_fibonacci,
    bench_fibonacci_comparison,
    bench_sorting,
    bench_hashmap,
);
criterion_main!(benches);

// Run: cargo bench
// HTML report: target/criterion/report/index.html
// Compare with previous: automatic — criterion saves baselines
```

instruction: How do I generate and interpret flame graphs for Rust programs to find performance bottlenecks?
output:
<think>
Flame graphs for Rust on Linux use `perf` + `inferno` (or `flamegraph` crate). The workflow: (1) compile with debug symbols (`CARGO_PROFILE_RELEASE_DEBUG=true` or `debug = true` in release profile), (2) run with `perf record`, (3) convert to flame graph with `inferno-flamegraph` or the `cargo flamegraph` shortcut.

`cargo flamegraph` is the easiest entry point — it's a single command that compiles, runs with perf, and generates the SVG. But understanding the underlying `perf` commands helps for complex scenarios (attaching to running processes, filtering by thread, etc.).

Reading Rust flame graphs: (1) Look for wide frames — that's where time is spent. (2) Rust names are mangled, but `inferno` demangles them. (3) `alloc::` frames indicate allocation overhead. (4) `core::fmt::` frames mean formatting is expensive. (5) `hashbrown::` frames suggest HashMap is a bottleneck. (6) Frames labeled `[unknown]` mean debug info is missing.

For allocation profiling specifically, use `dhat` (DHAT valgrind replacement in Rust) which counts allocations and shows where memory is allocated.
</think>

```rust
// === Setup: Cargo.toml ===
// [profile.release]
// debug = true  # REQUIRED for meaningful flame graphs

// === Method 1: cargo flamegraph (simplest) ===
// Install: cargo install flamegraph
// Run: cargo flamegraph --bin my_app -- arg1 arg2
// Output: flamegraph.svg

// === Method 2: Manual perf + inferno ===
// cargo install inferno
// cargo build --release
// perf record -g --call-graph dwarf target/release/my_app
// perf script | inferno-collapse-perf | inferno-flamegraph > flame.svg

// === Example: Code with intentional bottlenecks to profile ===
use std::collections::HashMap;

fn main() {
    let data = generate_data(1_000_000);
    let processed = process_data(&data);       // hot function
    let aggregated = aggregate(&processed);     // allocation-heavy
    let formatted = format_results(&aggregated); // formatting overhead
    println!("Results: {} entries", formatted.len());
}

// This will show as a wide bar in the flame graph
fn process_data(data: &[Record]) -> Vec<ProcessedRecord> {
    data.iter()
        .filter(|r| r.value > 50.0)
        .map(|r| ProcessedRecord {
            key: r.key.to_uppercase(),  // String allocation per record
            score: (r.value * 1.5).sqrt(),
        })
        .collect()
}

// HashMap operations will show as hashbrown:: frames
fn aggregate(records: &[ProcessedRecord]) -> HashMap<String, f64> {
    let mut map = HashMap::new();
    for record in records {
        *map.entry(record.key.clone()).or_insert(0.0) += record.score;
    }
    map
}

// fmt:: frames in the flame graph
fn format_results(data: &HashMap<String, f64>) -> Vec<String> {
    data.iter()
        .map(|(k, v)| format!("{}: {:.2}", k, v))  // format! allocates
        .collect()
}

struct Record { key: String, value: f64 }
struct ProcessedRecord { key: String, score: f64 }

fn generate_data(n: usize) -> Vec<Record> {
    (0..n).map(|i| Record {
        key: format!("key_{}", i % 1000),
        value: (i as f64) * 0.1,
    }).collect()
}

// === Reading the flame graph ===
// Wide bars at top: leaf functions consuming CPU
//   - "to_uppercase" → String processing is expensive
//   - "alloc::" → too many allocations
//   - "hashbrown::" → HashMap is hot
// Wide bars at bottom: callers spending time in children
//   - "process_data" → main bottleneck
// Fix: pre-allocate, use &str instead of String, use FxHashMap
```

instruction: How do I track memory allocations in Rust to find where my program is allocating too much?
output:
<think>
Rust's default allocator doesn't track allocations. To profile allocations, you have several options:

1. `dhat` crate — Rust port of Valgrind's DHAT tool. Wraps the global allocator to count allocations, track sizes, and show allocation sites. Zero external dependencies. Best for development.

2. Custom global allocator that counts allocations — simple wrapper around System allocator with atomic counters. Useful for production monitoring.

3. `jemalloc` with `malloc_stats` — if using jemallocator, you can dump allocation statistics.

4. Valgrind/Massif on Linux — external tool, heavy but thorough.

The `dhat` approach is the most practical for Rust. You add `#[global_allocator] static ALLOC: dhat::Alloc = dhat::Alloc;` and wrap main in a `dhat::Profiler`. It generates a JSON file viewable in Firefox's DHAT viewer (about:profiling or the online viewer).

For finding allocation-heavy code without external tools, you can write a counting allocator wrapper. Increment an atomic counter on every alloc/dealloc, then log it at key points.
</think>

```rust
// === Method 1: dhat — allocation profiler ===
// Cargo.toml: dhat = "0.3"

#[cfg(feature = "dhat")]
#[global_allocator]
static ALLOC: dhat::Alloc = dhat::Alloc;

fn main() {
    #[cfg(feature = "dhat")]
    let _profiler = dhat::Profiler::new_heap();

    // Your program here — dhat tracks every allocation
    let result = allocate_heavy_work();
    println!("Result: {}", result.len());

    // On drop, _profiler writes dhat-heap.json
    // View at: https://nnethercote.github.io/dh_view/dh_view.html
}

fn allocate_heavy_work() -> Vec<String> {
    (0..100_000)
        .map(|i| format!("item_{}", i))  // 100k String allocations
        .collect()
}

// === Method 2: Counting allocator wrapper ===
use std::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout, System};
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};

struct CountingAllocator {
    inner: System,
    alloc_count: AtomicUsize,
    dealloc_count: AtomicUsize,
    bytes_allocated: AtomicUsize,
}

unsafe impl GlobalAlloc for CountingAllocator {
    unsafe fn alloc(&self, layout: Layout) -> *mut u8 {
        self.alloc_count.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        self.bytes_allocated.fetch_add(layout.size(), Ordering::Relaxed);
        unsafe { self.inner.alloc(layout) }
    }

    unsafe fn dealloc(&self, ptr: *mut u8, layout: Layout) {
        self.dealloc_count.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        self.bytes_allocated.fetch_sub(layout.size(), Ordering::Relaxed);
        unsafe { self.inner.dealloc(ptr, layout) }
    }
}

impl CountingAllocator {
    const fn new() -> Self {
        CountingAllocator {
            inner: System,
            alloc_count: AtomicUsize::new(0),
            dealloc_count: AtomicUsize::new(0),
            bytes_allocated: AtomicUsize::new(0),
        }
    }

    fn stats(&self) -> (usize, usize, usize) {
        (
            self.alloc_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed),
            self.dealloc_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed),
            self.bytes_allocated.load(Ordering::Relaxed),
        )
    }

    fn reset(&self) {
        self.alloc_count.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
        self.dealloc_count.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
    }
}

// #[global_allocator]
// static COUNTING: CountingAllocator = CountingAllocator::new();

// Usage:
// COUNTING.reset();
// do_work();
// let (allocs, deallocs, bytes) = COUNTING.stats();
// println!("Allocations: {allocs}, Live bytes: {bytes}");
```

instruction: How do I use perf and flamegraph to profile a Rust binary that's CPU-bound on Linux?
output:
<think>
perf is the Linux kernel's profiling tool. For Rust, the workflow is: compile with debug symbols in release mode, run under perf record, then generate a flame graph from the perf data.

Critical setup: (1) `debug = true` in `[profile.release]` — without this, you get `[unknown]` frames everywhere. (2) `perf record -g --call-graph dwarf` — dwarf gives better stack traces than frame pointers for Rust code. (3) If using LTO, stacks may be incomplete because inlined functions disappear.

For `perf stat`, you get hardware counter data: cache misses, branch mispredictions, IPC (instructions per cycle). This tells you whether the bottleneck is compute-bound, memory-bound, or branch-bound — information that flame graphs can't show.

`perf annotate` shows assembly-level hotspots within a function. This is useful when the flame graph points to one function but you need to know which loop or instruction is hot.

The `cargo flamegraph` crate wraps all of this into one command, but understanding perf directly is important for attaching to running processes or using advanced features.
</think>

```rust
// Step 1: Cargo.toml — enable debug symbols in release
// [profile.release]
// debug = true
// # opt-level = 3  (default for release, keep it)

// Step 2: Build
// cargo build --release

// Step 3: Record with perf
// sudo perf record -g --call-graph dwarf -F 99 target/release/my_app
// -g: collect call graphs
// --call-graph dwarf: use DWARF debug info (best for Rust)
// -F 99: sample at 99Hz (avoids synchronization with timers)

// Step 4: Generate flame graph
// sudo perf script | inferno-collapse-perf | inferno-flamegraph > flame.svg

// === Alternative: perf stat for hardware counters ===
// sudo perf stat -e cache-misses,cache-references,branch-misses,instructions,cycles \
//   target/release/my_app
//
// Output:
//   1,234,567  cache-misses   #  3.45% of cache references
//  35,789,012  cache-references
//     456,789  branch-misses  #  0.12% of branches
// 987,654,321  instructions   #  2.34 insn per cycle (IPC)
// 421,234,567  cycles

// === Example: CPU-bound code to profile ===
fn main() {
    let n = 10_000_000;
    let data: Vec<f64> = (0..n).map(|i| (i as f64) * 0.001).collect();

    // CPU-bound: math-heavy computation
    let result: f64 = compute_heavy(&data);
    println!("Result: {:.6}", result);

    // Memory-bound: random access pattern
    let lookup_result = random_access_pattern(&data);
    println!("Lookup: {:.6}", lookup_result);
}

// Will show high IPC in perf stat (compute-bound)
fn compute_heavy(data: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    data.iter()
        .map(|x| x.sin() * x.cos() + x.exp().ln())
        .sum()
}

// Will show high cache-miss rate in perf stat (memory-bound)
fn random_access_pattern(data: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let mut sum = 0.0;
    let mut idx = 0usize;
    for _ in 0..data.len() {
        sum += data[idx];
        idx = ((idx * 6364136223846793005 + 1) % data.len()) as usize;
    }
    sum
}

// === perf annotate: assembly-level view ===
// sudo perf annotate --symbol=compute_heavy
// Shows assembly with per-instruction sample counts
// Look for hot instructions (high sample count) to identify bottleneck
```
